category_title: Sixth-grade students with LD who doodle and lack orientation to the teacher during math
minimal_context: Chris is working one-on-one with a teacher during after-school homework support to complete his math problem-solving assignment.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Let's get started on your math homework. Please complete the first problem-solving procedure on the worksheet.

STUDENT: (Digs through backpack) I can't find my math notebook or my pencil.

TUTOR: Here is a pencil and some paper. Go ahead and try the first problem.

STUDENT: (Draws a picture of a basketball on the corner of the paper) I don't remember how we did this in class today.

TUTOR: Look at the problem again and see what you can figure out.

STUDENT: (Pushes the paper away) It's too hard and I keep making mistakes. Math is just not my subject.

options.A: Use the MARS template during home room period to promote self-evaluation by asking him to rate how well he did with each letter and record one area he could improve.
options.B: Draw on culturally sustaining pedagogies to ensure that he is solving math problems about a topic that matters to him during the homework session.
options.C: Tell him the expectations ahead of time so he will be less tempted to escalate his doodling to steal your attention during the homework session.
options.D: Require him either to rewrite the fraction as a multiplication problem or write down the amount by which he is dividing both the numerator and denominator.
